item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When you wish upon a tiger plush, all your dreams will come true.
until the next day usually.
+1

item.title: Re: Monkey's Paw
item.content:encoded: You'd probably have a $20 bill you can't spend due to it's sticking to your body, forever, or in worst case you might have only $20 dollars total paper money for the rest of your life, ATM malfunctioning only giving you $20 bucks no matter how much you typed or in your account, the hundred bucks you borrowed from Steven next door turned into $20 bill and he found $80 back in his wallet.

item.title: Monkey's Paw
item.content:encoded: So, it is a semi-wishmaker? Well, what happens if you wish to have it forever? Let us say for example, instead of saying, I want $20, I said, "I want $20 in USD paper money forever." Will I get to keep it? Is it like in the Monkey's paw where you have to be VERY specific on how you want your wish to turn out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the Mediocre Monkey's Paw. I've always been fond of those kind of stories, and spent much time dealing with D&D GM's who cackled with glee whenever we found a ring of wishes…Well done. Never wish for a surprise. Wish for a pleasant surprise.

+1. Because God fucking damn it, everything tastes like broccoli!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed. The formatting was weird, which resulted in lines being missing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Withholding my upvote till the odd bit in the second test is fixed. there's no result area

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it well enough but you didn't include the actual result line for the dog test. Seems to be an oversight from revision because it was there in past versions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eric's sister?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Faded "Sarah" on care and cleaning instructions tag makes me sad.

I'm curious about how Sarah lived before and after containment of 1990. I feel pity for her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you wanted to create one, you'd need to get the original author's permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This needs a test log page, I really think lots of very fun things could be done with this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I already liked the concept of this SCP, but the little log you made supported it and gave me a little chuckle. Good job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There should be a wish tag. We have a decent amount of wish-granters at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You sir/madam, have my upvote simply for the corgi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. Read this a while back, wasn't blown away, re-read it, don't really like it at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I for one rather like it. It's a bit half-hearted, yeah, but still good and within reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's funny and original for a wish granter. Pretty mediocre overall though. Vote witheld.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of a child having this is intriguing enough for me to give it a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess we pretty much got what was advertised: a mediocre wish giver, emphasis on mediocre.

The overall article is pretty bland, and while the test logs showed a glimmer of something interesting, the last two tests sent it over the edge into bad.

Overall, downvote, but I would like to see this concept done right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon review, I'm not over-fond of this one anymore.

But as far as wish-granters go it's original enough, and makes me chuckle. I don't want to damn it specifically, so I'm removing an old +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts were already given above. This is abysmal.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This article is being placed up for review. Please take the time to come check it out, it could use more attention.

I found this one to be… well, pretty mediocre. The actual effect doesn't really do much for me, and the test logs made me cringe with how poorly put together they are. This just doesn't feel particularly thought out or well put together. If the test logs could be made anything but silly and ridiculous, it might make up for it, but I really don't like it as-is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ugh. Concept is silly and test logs weren't very interesting. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Due to no D-class personnel dying that day, hundreds of SCP experiments were ruined, several doctors were killed, [DATA EXPUNGED], and the tests had to be repeated. Dr.███████ was given a disciplinary hearing and was deemed un-fit to do further testing of SCP-1990 for the time being."

This bugged me, massively. It is incredibly implausible that a single day of no D-class dying would ruin hundreds of experiments. D-class are a very limited resource, there's no way the foundation kills hundreds of them daily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Test logs +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wished for a new SCP last night. D:

But seriously, this is pretty meh. The loldenda made me cringe and doctors shouldn't test directly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nearly all of the posts on here made me laugh til I fell over. I find the whole article funny and quirky, and would upvote it no matter if it were a joke or on the main page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I second that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept interests me, and the testing logs are funny. I like how quirky the item in general is. Good job! Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This might be better reworked as a -J, with Dr. Blackbox's as Site Director James. His wishes are all very childish. "I want a puppy!" "I don't want any D-Class to die." "I want a surprise!" "Nothing should taste like broccoli."

I really like the Deutchsmark test, the surprise test, and the broccoli test, but they're all pretty jokey. And someone needs to take a good shot at tone and grammar editing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed with this one, i thought of it too. Maybe make it a non-researcher of some kind…it's stretching it, maybe just be done with the surprise completely.

Otherwise, it was interesting and fun to read. Upvote.

EDIT: Read SCP-274. Retracting the upvote, that one did it better. You need to work on the tone and also the fact that no sane researcher would do such a wish on D-Class. What if we get a bunch of invulnerable D-Class around? Bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But remember the time limit on living beings. The first deaths were caused by the production of inanimate objects; the second were caused by a direct effect on living D-class. In other words, if she'd wished for a hippopotamus and it landed on her, she'd be back the next day after the hippo disappeared.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the object, but the extent of the wish granting seems a bit dramatic for what is basically a wimpy genie. It may very well be intentional, but for my 2c, having it able to drastically alter reality kinda breaks the suspension of disbelief for me.

For instance:

Wish: Subject wished that no D-class personnel would die tomorrow from other SCP experiments.
Result: No D-class personnel died that day.

Addendum: Due to no D-class personnel dying that day, hundreds of SCP experiments were ruined, several doctors were killed, [DATA EXPUNGED], and the tests had to be repeated. Dr.███████ was given a disciplinary hearing and was deemed un-fit to do further testing of SCP-1990 for the time being.

Second addendum: The next day, all D-class personnel who should have died that day, all died according to what would have normally happened to them under each SCP's effects at the specific times of testing. In addition, the doctors who died from that as well were found returning to work the next day with no memories of the previous tests. Dr.███████ was re-instated to continue testing SCP-1990.

in my mind should have been something more like:

Wish: Subject wished that no D-class personnel would die tomorrow from other SCP experiments.
Result: All D-class due for testing were listed as F-class across all Foundation locations

Other than that, I really enjoy this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish it were possible to upvote comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, but virtually every object in Foundation custody would have killed or disabled the first five or six people who interacted with it until they figured them out. If Foundation doctors dealt directly with objects on a regular basis, before their properties were known, that conversation would really go like this:

"So what? Get Doc Y for the plants, Doc Z for the physics, and recruit that grad student whose thesis on Sanskrit erotica we flagged last week."

"Yeah, well, Doc Y got turned to stone in that containment breach yesterday, Z is only 5 cm tall, and all the recruiters currently think they're iguanas because of that memetic flash drive the GoC left in the parking lot on Tuesday."

"Shit. Well how about Doc V and Doc W?"

"Flayed and eaten by that folding chair in the Thursday containment breach, and being held hostage at Site 16 by sapient millipedes."

"Doc U?"

"Present and healthy, but non-corporeal."

"Doc T? S? R?"

"Drowned when that tetherball started spewing honey everywhere, growing red flowers from every orifice of his body, speaks only Tagalog until the effects wear off in four or five days."

"Well shit, do we have anybody?"

"There's you and me."

"I don't know anything about botany, particle physics, or Sanskrit! I'm a pediatrician, for fuck's sakes!"

"Exactly. Also, don't freak out or anything, but there's a humidifier sneaking up behind you."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Testing is integral to containment.
We must know as much as possible about an object in order to figure out what will stop it from getting out.
In this case, if there was no testing and containment was based only on initial information, then this would be treated as a massively dangerous, high-priority item that could potentailly destroy all of reality. Because on day 1, they just thought it granted wishes. What happens if someone wishes for "world peace"? The tiger could just kill everybody.
But because of proper scientific testing, we know that this is nothing to worry about. Because A) it doesn't grant wishes very well, and B) it can't screw with living things past a 24 hour mark.

As for staff testing, I don't see the problem. A Foundation doctor risks death, madness, and literally unimaginable consequences just by coming to work. The extra risk incurred from interacting with an item directly is statistically insignificant. And I don't think that they are irreplaceable.

"Well, Doc X died testing out scp-####, now we don't have anyone on staff with degrees in botany and particle physics who reads sanskrit."
"So what? Get Doc Y for the plants, Doc Z for the physics, and recruit that grad student whos thesis on sanskrit erotica we flagged last week."

Not to mention that out-of-universe, testing is entertaining. I will forgive a lot of things that are nonsense in-universe if the result is entertaining to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hrm….
The girl's wish was presumably something like "I wish my parents had lots of money", the tiger produced bills, the bills then suffocated the parents.
The Doc's wish was "No d-class die today", the tiger produced altered circumstances to save d-class that would have died, altered circumstances then kill staffers.
It seems to me that both sets of fatalities are similarly indirect, and should both either be reversed or not, if directness is the criteria.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh. now i feel even more dumb. STOP MAKING ME FEEL DUMB SCANTRON.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's why I said I felt flattered. To be clear, I wrote 066, not 1990. I would never post anything this dumb. I would never post an SCP intended to be taken seriously that was this dumb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh no. I also didn't realize you were the one who wrote it. Now I feel dumb. Ima go cry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I got that reference, being the one who wrote it. I was just thinking there was another reference in the girl being specified as Sarah and the tiger eating her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Before he could be provided with further instruction, D-066-4437 attempted to cut it again, only to have SCP-066 roll away again and produce the phrase “Are you Eric?”. After D-066-4437 replied in the negative, SCP-066 morphed into its present state (see file photo) and began emitting loud, dissonant staccato notes until D-066-4437 was escorted from the room."

There was another one with marbles, but i think it got down voted to oblivion. Basically when you mentioned a girl who has an abnormal plaything, I immediately thought of Eric.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hehehehe. I'm flattered, even if I don't understand the reference entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't dislike this, but it feels like something I would want to have, as long as I didn't ask for something stupid like people coming back to life or anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On ██/██/████ SCP-1990 vocalized unexpectedly, asking Dr. ████ if he was "Sarah" when Dr. ████ replied in the negative SCP-1990 grew to a full grown tiger and ate him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This pretty much encapsulates why I downvoted.

Actually, no. In addition to the stuff Scantron mentioned, I'm having a very difficult time with the fact that staff is allowed to test this at all. I've read a few articles lately where Foundation staff are lining up to interact with the anomalous object, which is reminiscent of older articles, but makes NO GODDAMN SENSE.

I mean, the things the Foundation is containing do not behave the way logic says they should. We very carefully lay out procedures so that even seemingly-safe objects don't do something bad. Why in the hell would the Foundation let researchers and doctors, who are highly-qualified and hard to replace assets, interact with something that has the demonstrated ability to do bad stuff to you?

If D-Class can't test it, it probably shouldn't be tested and just contained instead. But I don't buy that D-Class can't test this. Get a test subject, don't tell him what the object does, and instruct him to read off a prepared script. Put fail-safe measures in place; he has a small bomb implanted in him that's triggered to go off if he's outside the facility, a trained sniper hidden from view has him in sight, etc. There's ways you could have at least attempted to get around this if you thought about it.

Also:

Wish: Subject wished that no D-class personnel would die tomorrow from other SCP experiments.
Result: No D-class personnel died that day.

Addendum: Due to no D-class personnel dying that day, hundreds of SCP experiments were ruined, several doctors were killed, [DATA EXPUNGED], and the tests had to be repeated. Dr.███████ was given a disciplinary hearing and was deemed un-fit to do further testing of SCP-1990 for the time being.

This makes no goddamn sense whatsoever. This "wish" had predictably stupid results; why was Dr. Blackbox allowed to do this in the first place? If it was incompetent enough to merit a disciplinary hearing afterwards, it was too stupid to be allowed by the researchers designing the experiment. No way at least one person in the Foundation doesn't say "hey, wait a minute" when they read this proposal.

Finally, there's a place for lolfoundation. It's in the -Js and the author pages. It does not belong in the mainlist. These are supposed to be clinical documents. Scientists may send each other humorous notes or say funny things in real life, but they don't write that shit up in the reports. It kills any suspension of disbelief I have, which frankly wasn't really there in the first place because of the quality of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I share the sentiment. Twisting a wish is one of the oldest tropes out there, and this skip doesn't do anything interesting with the idea. It just wraps it in a slightly different bow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is not a very interesting idea. It's just another thing that grants wishes in bad ways. It's been done before, and much better. It's also poorly written, for all the reasons Scantron mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's a monkey's paw with a mildly humorous test log… I don't really see the attraction. Also, "mediocre" really isn't appropriately clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Guh. Wish-fulfillment WITH A TWEEEEEST needs to be done really well to work for me.

SCP-1990 requires no specific containment procedures at this time

So I'm allowed to hump the SCP, right? Or did you mean that it doesn't require extensive containment procedures?

Newspapers had reported a strange incident in which a man and a woman had died from suffocation, the cause of which was found to be burial under a large pile of 1925-issue Deutschmarks.

The young girl was in a distressed state and was holding SCP-1990.

Okay, the Foundation would mark this incident as odd. But there's a pretty big leap from "odd thing happens to couple" to "the kid slept while holding the pet tiger, which grants wishes WITH A TWEEEEEST

The couple had earlier reported that they were having money issues.

To who?

Upon awakening, a mediocre version of the wished item or concept will occur.

Define 'mediocre' in a manner that sounds like a scientist wrote this report.

As for the test logs in general, the effect seems really, really inconsistent. In order,

  1. It grants the wish only in a particularly abstract manner.
  2. It grants the wish, and then undoes the wish, except the memory of the results.
  3. It grants the wish, lolfoundation happens, and an arbitrary effect is added to the SCP.
  4. It grants the wish in a sadistic manner.
  5. It grants the wish and adds in a smartass effect.

So in short, this does not feel well thought-out or consistent in effect, and the writing ain't that great. I have no idea why it's positively rated.

Side note: For crying out loud, it's just wish fulfillment WITH A TWEEEEEST!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is extremely mediocre.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: More the other way around, I think. The deaths of the researchers were part of the direct effect; they died because the D-classes didn't. The deaths of the parents were indirect; rather than being part of the wish, they were a side effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edward Eager. I loved the hell out of that book when I was a kid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one, but I have one question. This SCP seems to have only a temporary effect on living beings, yet it seems to have killed the child's parents permanently. Did they die because it was a direct effect of SCP-1990 (burying them under a pile of money), while the researchers who died because the D-Class didn't was because it was an indirect effect of the "wish"? I sort of wish the parents had come back to life as well. Still, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit, the testing logs made me giggle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hah…I like this. It reminds me of a book I read a while ago called "Half Magic" by an author whose name I can't remember. It was a good book, and the "wish" thing functioned a lot like this.

Anyway, don't mind my ramblings, take my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-class personnel would be too volatile or not trustworthy enough to be used to test SCP-1990. Dr. ███████ has been assigned to carry out all testing of SCP-1990.

The fact that no testing should be performed with D-class should probably go in the containment procedures.

It might be a good idea to add psychological screening requirements as well given the open ended nature of the item and the amount of trust that has to be on the person performing the tests.

Just theorizing here, but given that the wishing has to be done while falling asleep could the mediocre nature of the granted wishes be due to the person doing the requesting falling asleep and muddling things up?
